answer:
**There is no line in "Tarana-e-Milli" (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 23) where Iqbal explicitly calls the Muslim community a "single body" (or equivalent phrasing like "one body," using terms such as *jism*, *tan*, or similar in the transliteration/original).**[^1][^2]

The poem strongly emphasizes the **unity of the global Muslim *ummah*** (community), portraying it as a **single, indivisible entity** spanning "Chin to Arab lands" with "the whole wide world's the home a Muslim knows" (*Cheen o Arab hamara, Hindustaan hamara / Muslim hain hum, watan hai saara jahan hamara*).[^3][^4] This opens the poem and recurs as a refrain-like motif of transnational oneness under *Tauheed* (the Oneness of God), but it uses homeland (*watan*) and guardianship (*paasbaan*) imagery rather than "body." Corpus-wide searches for related concepts ("single body," "one body," "ummah," "millat," "unity") confirmed no literal match.[^5]

If this refers to a specific translation variant or paraphrase outside the Falsafa corpus (e.g., a common interpretive gloss of *ummah* as a "body politic"), it is not present here. The corpus's Urdu original, transliteration, and English translation lack it.

[^1]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 23 "Tarana-e-Milli" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/).
[^2]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 23 "Tarana-e-Milli" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/transliteration/).
[^3]: [p-8a4681](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/#p-8a4681).
[^4]: [p-9b6133](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/transliteration/#p-9b6133).
[^5]: Corpus search results for ""single body" OR "one body" iqbal muslim"; ""muslim" "body" OR "ummah" OR "millat" OR "one" OR "single" OR "united" OR "unity" iqbal".
